section: Chapter 3: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

concepts.lesson: A linear equation in two variables is an equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a and b are real numbers and are not both zero. Such an equation represents a straight line when plotted on a Cartesian plane. Each solution of the equation corresponds to a point on this line, and since a straight line contains infinitely many points, a linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions. This concept forms the foundation for understanding systems of equations and graphical methods in algebra.

The graphical solution of a pair of linear equations involves plotting both equations on the same coordinate plane and observing their point of intersection. The coordinates of the intersection point, if it exists, represent the solution of the system. If the lines intersect at exactly one point, there is a unique solution. If the lines are parallel, they never meet and hence there is no solution. If the lines coincide, every point on the line satisfies both equations, leading to infinitely many solutions. This method provides a visual understanding of the nature of solutions.
concepts.real_example: Intersecting lines meet at one point.

concepts.lesson: A pair of linear equations is said to be consistent if it has at least one solution, and inconsistent if it has no solution. Consistency depends on the relative position of the lines represented by the equations. Intersecting lines and coincident lines form consistent systems, while parallel lines form an inconsistent system. Analysing consistency helps in understanding whether a given system of equations can be solved meaningfully and what type of solution can be expected.
concepts.real_example: Intersecting lines form a consistent system.

concepts.lesson: Algebraic methods provide systematic techniques for solving a pair of linear equations without relying on graphs. The substitution method involves expressing one variable in terms of the other and substituting it into the second equation. The elimination method removes one variable by adding or subtracting equations after making coefficients equal. The cross-multiplication method offers a direct formula-based approach. These methods are efficient, precise, and widely used in examinations.
concepts.real_example: Solving x + y = 5 and x − y = 1.

concepts.lesson: The nature of solutions of a pair of linear equations depends on the ratios of their coefficients. If a₁/a₂ ≠ b₁/b₂, the lines intersect and there is a unique solution. If a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂, the lines are parallel and there is no solution. If a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂, the lines coincide and there are infinitely many solutions. These conditions allow the nature of solutions to be determined without drawing graphs, making them useful for analytical and exam-based problems.
concepts.real_example: Parallel lines when a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂.
